subject: Physics
q: Let <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mi> </mi><mo>+</mo><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>)</mo></math> . The magnitude of a coplanar vector <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> such that <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mi> </mi><mo>.</mo><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>.</mo><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mi> </mi><mo>.</mo><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is given by:
question: Let <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mi> </mi><mo>+</mo><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>)</mo></math> . The magnitude of a coplanar vector <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> such that <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mi> </mi><mo>.</mo><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>.</mo><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mi> </mi><mo>.</mo><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is given by:
solution:
If C→=ai^ + bj^ then, 
A→ . C→=A→ . B→  
a+b=1  .......(i)
B→. C→= A→ . B→
2a-b=1  ......(ii)
solving equations (i) and (ii) we get, 
a=13, b=23
C→= 19+49= 59

subject: Physics
q:
A particle is moving with a velocity v→=Kyi^+xj^, where K is a constant.

The general equation for its path is:
question:
A particle is moving with a velocity v→=Kyi^+xj^, where K is a constant.

The general equation for its path is:
options: <math><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></math>

<math><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></math>

<math><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></math>

<math><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></math>
solution:
v→=Kyi^+xj^

vx=y=dxdt

vy=x=dydt

dxdy=yx

∫x dx=∫y dy

x22=y22+C

or y2=x2+constant

subject: Physics
q: When vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> is subtracted from vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math>, it gives a vector equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math>. Then the magnitude of vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> will be:
question: When vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> is subtracted from vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math>, it gives a vector equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math>. Then the magnitude of vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> will be:
solution:
It is given that A→=2i^+3j^+2k^.

By the given condition,

 B→-(2i^+3j^+2k^)=2j^

 ⇒B→=2i^+5j^+2k^ 

The magnitude is given by the formula,B→=B2x+B2y+B2z
 ⇒|B→|=4+25+4=33

subject: Physics
q: Resistance of a given wire is obtained by measuring the current flowing in it and the voltage difference applied across it. If the percentage errors in the measurement of the current and the voltage difference are $3 \%$ each, then error in the value of resistance of the wire is
question: Resistance of a given wire is obtained by measuring the current flowing in it and the voltage difference applied across it. If the percentage errors in the measurement of the current and the voltage difference are $3 \%$ each, then error in the value of resistance of the wire is
solution:
$\mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{i}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad\left|\frac{\Delta \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{R}}\right|=\left|\frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}}\right|+\left|\frac{\Delta \mathrm{i}}{\mathrm{i}}\right|$
$\frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}} \times 100=3$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}}=0.03$
Similarly, $\frac{\Delta i}{i}=0.03$
Hence $\frac{\Delta \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{R}}=0.06$
So percentage error is $\frac{\Delta \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{R}} \times 100=6 \%$

subject: Physics
q: Two vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>P</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>Q</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> have equal magnitudes. If the magnitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>P</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi>Q</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> times the magnitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>P</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mover><mi>Q</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>,</mo></math> then angle between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>P</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>Q</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is
question: Two vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>P</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>Q</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> have equal magnitudes. If the magnitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>P</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi>Q</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> times the magnitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>P</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mover><mi>Q</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>,</mo></math> then angle between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>P</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>Q</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is
solution:
|P→|=|Q→|=x  …i

|P→+Q→|=n|P→-Q→|

P2+Q2+2PQcosθ=n2P2+Q2-2PQcosθ

Using (i) in above equation,

cosθ=n2-11+n2

θ=cos-1n2-1n2+1

subject: Physics
q: The diameter and height of a cylinder are measured by a meter scale to be <math><mn>12.6</mn><mo>±</mo><mn>0.1</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math> and <math><mn>34.2</mn><mo>±</mo><mn>0.1</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math> , respectively. What will be the value of its volume in appropriate significant figures?
question: The diameter and height of a cylinder are measured by a meter scale to be <math><mn>12.6</mn><mo>±</mo><mn>0.1</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math> and <math><mn>34.2</mn><mo>±</mo><mn>0.1</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math> , respectively. What will be the value of its volume in appropriate significant figures?
solution:
V=πd2h4=π412.6234.2 cm3

dVV=2ddd+dhh

Converting to errors

∆VV=2∆dd+∆hh

∆V4260=20.112.6+0.134.2

⇒∆V=80 cm3

V=4260±80 cm3

subject: Physics
q: Three forces F1=10 N, F2=8 N, F3=6 N are acting on a particle of mass 5 kg. The forces F2 and F3 are applied perpendicularly so that particle remains at rest. If the force F1 is removed, then the acceleration of the particle is
question: Three forces F1=10 N, F2=8 N, F3=6 N are acting on a particle of mass 5 kg. The forces F2 and F3 are applied perpendicularly so that particle remains at rest. If the force F1 is removed, then the acceleration of the particle is
solution:
When the force F1 is removed, the particle starts accelerating because of the resultant force between F2 and F3.

The resultant force can be calculated as follows:

F=F22+F32=10 N

Hence, the acceleration of the particle is given by

a=10 N5 kg=2 m s–2

subject: Physics
q: Two forces are such that the sum of their magnitudes is $18 \mathrm{~N}$ and their resultant is $12 \mathrm{~N}$ which is perpendicular to the smaller force. Then the magnitudes of the forces are
question: Two forces are such that the sum of their magnitudes is $18 \mathrm{~N}$ and their resultant is $12 \mathrm{~N}$ which is perpendicular to the smaller force. Then the magnitudes of the forces are
solution:
Use $\tan \alpha=\frac{P \sin \theta}{Q+P \cos \theta} \Rightarrow \tan 90^{\circ}=\frac{P \sin \theta}{Q+P \cos \theta}=\infty$
$\therefore Q+P \cos \theta=0 \Rightarrow P \cos \theta=-Q$
$\mathrm{R}=\sqrt{\mathrm{P}^2+\mathrm{Q}^2+2 \mathrm{PQ} \cos \theta} \mathrm{R}=\sqrt{\mathrm{P}^2+\mathrm{Q}^2-2 \mathrm{Q}^2}$ or $\mathrm{R}=\sqrt{\mathrm{P}^2-\mathrm{Q}^2}=12$
$144=(\mathrm{P}+\mathrm{Q})(\mathrm{P}-\mathrm{Q})$ or $\mathrm{P}-\mathrm{Q}=144 / 18=8 \quad \therefore \mathrm{P}=13 \mathrm{~N}$ and $\mathrm{Q}=5 \mathrm{~N}$

subject: Physics
q: In an experiment of determine the Young's modulus of wire of a length exactly <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>, the extension in the length of the wire is measured as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> with an uncertainty of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>±</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> when a load of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is applied. The diameter of the wire is measured as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> with an uncertainty of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>±</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The error in the measurement of Young's modulus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>Δ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
question: In an experiment of determine the Young's modulus of wire of a length exactly <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>, the extension in the length of the wire is measured as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> with an uncertainty of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>±</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> when a load of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is applied. The diameter of the wire is measured as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> with an uncertainty of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>±</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The error in the measurement of Young's modulus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>Δ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
solution:
Given here, 

Length, L=1 m and extension in length,  ΔL=0.4×10-3 m

Mass of load, m=1 kg and diameter of wire, d=0.4×10-3 m

Young's modulus is given by

Y=FLAΔL=mg×1πd240.4×10-3

⇒Y=10×4π0.4×10-32×0.4×10-3

⇒Y=40×722×64×10-3×10-9   

⇒Y=0.199×10-12 N m-2

Error in measurement of Young's modulus,

ΔYY=ΔFF+ΔLL+ΔAA+ΔΔLΔL

=0.020.4+2Δdd=0.24+2×0.010.4

=0.12+0.12=0.1

=0.199×1011≈2×1010

Hence, the value of x=2.

subject: Physics
q:
Assertion A: If A, B, C, D are four points on a semi-circular arc with a centre at O such that |AB→|=|BC→|=|CD→|. Then, AB→+AC→+AD→=4AO→+OB→+OC→

Reason R: Polygon law of vector addition yields AB→+BC→+CD→+AD→=2AO→

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
question:
Assertion A: If A, B, C, D are four points on a semi-circular arc with a centre at O such that |AB→|=|BC→|=|CD→|. Then, AB→+AC→+AD→=4AO→+OB→+OC→

Reason R: Polygon law of vector addition yields AB→+BC→+CD→+AD→=2AO→

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is not correct.
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is not correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is correct.

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are correct and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>.</mo></math>

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is not the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution: Polygon law is applicable in both but the equation given in the reason is not useful in explaining the assertion.

subject: Physics
q: Three particles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>Q</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are moving along the vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mo>  </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>C</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math>, respectively. They strike on a point and start to move in different directions. Now particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is moving normal to the plane which contains vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>.</mo></math> Similarly particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> is moving normal to the plane which contains vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>C</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>.</mo></math> The angle between the direction of motion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt></mfrac></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math> Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><menclose notation="bottom"><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo></menclose></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>.</mo></math>
question: Three particles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>Q</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are moving along the vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mo>  </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>C</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math>, respectively. They strike on a point and start to move in different directions. Now particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is moving normal to the plane which contains vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>.</mo></math> Similarly particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> is moving normal to the plane which contains vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>A</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>C</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>.</mo></math> The angle between the direction of motion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt></mfrac></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math> Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><menclose notation="bottom"><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo></menclose></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Direction of P, v^1=±A→×B→|A→×B→|=±i^-j^+k^3

Direction of Q, v^2=±A→×C→|A→×C→|=±2k^2=±k^

Angle between v^1 and v^2,

v^1·v^2v^1v^2=±13(1)(1)=±13

⇒x=3

subject: Physics
q: In an experiment to measure focal length $(f)$ of convex lens, the least counts of the measuring scales for the position of object $(\mathrm{u})$ and for the position of image $(\mathrm{v})$ are $\Delta \mathrm{u}$ and $\Delta \mathrm{v}$, respectively. The error in the measurement of the focal length of the convex lens will be:
question: In an experiment to measure focal length $(f)$ of convex lens, the least counts of the measuring scales for the position of object $(\mathrm{u})$ and for the position of image $(\mathrm{v})$ are $\Delta \mathrm{u}$ and $\Delta \mathrm{v}$, respectively. The error in the measurement of the focal length of the convex lens will be:
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}^{-1}=\mathrm{v}^{-1}-\mathrm{u}^{-1} \\ & -\mathrm{f}^{-2} \mathrm{df}=-\mathrm{v}^{-2} \mathrm{dv}-\mathrm{u}^{-2} \mathrm{du} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{df}}{\mathrm{f}^2}=\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{v}^2}+\frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{u}^2} \\ & \mathrm{df}=\mathrm{f}^2\left[\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{v}^2}+\frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{u}^2}\right]\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics

question: A body is moving with constant speed, in a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. subject: Physics
q: If the percentage errors in measuring the length and the diameter of a wire are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>%</mo></math> each. The percentage error in measuring its resistance will be:
question: If the percentage errors in measuring the length and the diameter of a wire are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>%</mo></math> each. The percentage error in measuring its resistance will be:
solution:
Resistance of the wire in terms of the diameter can be written as, R=ρLπd24

Hence, percentage error relation will be, ∆RR×100=∆LL×100+2∆dd×100

Given: ∆LL=0.1% and ∆dd=0.1%

Therefore, ∆RR=0.3%

Then the fractional error in the measurement of Y is :
question:
A student determined Young's Modulus of elasticity using the formula Y=MgL34bd3δ. The value of g is taken to be 9.8 m s-2 without any significant error, his observations are as following.

Physical Quantity

Least count of the Equipment used for measurement

 Observed Value
Mass (M) 1 g 2 kg
Length of bar (L) 1 mm 1 m
Breadth of bar (b) 0.1 mm 4 cm
Thickness of bar  (d) 0.01 mm 0.4 cm
Depression (δ) 0.01 mm 5 mm

Then the fractional error in the measurement of Y is :
solution:
Y=MgL34bd3δ

∆YY=∆MM+3∆l1+∆bb+3∆dd+∆δδ⇒∆YY=0.0005+3×0.001+0.0025+3×0.0025+0.002=0.0155

question: For an experimental expression $y=\frac{32.3 \times 1125}{27.4}$, where all the digits are significant. Then to report the value of $y$ we should write

subject: Physics
q: In an experiment the values of two spring constants were measured as $k_{1}=(10 \pm 0.2) \mathrm{N} / \mathrm{m}$ and $k_{2}=(20 \pm 0.3) \mathrm{N} / \mathrm{m}$. If these springs are connected in parallel, then the percentage error in equivalent spring constant is :
question: In an experiment the values of two spring constants were measured as $k_{1}=(10 \pm 0.2) \mathrm{N} / \mathrm{m}$ and $k_{2}=(20 \pm 0.3) \mathrm{N} / \mathrm{m}$. If these springs are connected in parallel, then the percentage error in equivalent spring constant is :
solution:
For parallel combination: $k_{eq} = k_1 + k_2 = 10 + 20 = 30$ N/m
For sum, absolute errors add:
$\Delta k_{eq} = \Delta k_1 + \Delta k_2 = 0.2 + 0.3 = 0.5$ N/m
Percentage error $= \frac{\Delta k_{eq}}{k_{eq}} \times 100 = \frac{0.5}{30} \times 100 = 1.67\%$

subject: Physics
q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>P</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>Q</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math>  then, the unit vector in the direction of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>P</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>×</mo><mover><mi>Q</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac><mo>(</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>)</mo></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>P</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>Q</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math>  then, the unit vector in the direction of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>P</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>×</mo><mover><mi>Q</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac><mo>(</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>)</mo></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
solution:
Unit vector in the direction of P→×Q→ is n^=P→×Q→|P→×Q→|.

Here, P→×Q→=i^j^k^332432.5=3i^2+j^2-3k^

And P→×Q→=322+122+32=4=2

⇒P→×Q→|P→×Q→|=123i^2+j^2-3k^

=14(3i^+j^-23k^)  ⇒x=4

q: A physical quantity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math> is represented by the formula <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>r</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mi mathvariant="italic">g</mi></mrow><mi mathvariant="italic">x</mi></msup><msup><mi>l</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></msup></math> If the percentage errors found in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">,</mo><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mi>m</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">,</mo><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mi>r</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">,</mo><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mi>l</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> respectively, then find the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math>.
question: A physical quantity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math> is represented by the formula <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>r</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mi mathvariant="italic">g</mi></mrow><mi mathvariant="italic">x</mi></msup><msup><mi>l</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></msup></math> If the percentage errors found in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">,</mo><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mi>m</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">,</mo><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mi>r</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">,</mo><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mi>l</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> respectively, then find the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math>.
solution:
Δyy=2Δmm+4Δrr+xΔgg+32Δll

18=2(1)+4(0.5)+xp+32(4)

8=xp

By checking from options.

x=163, p=±32

subject: Physics
q: Three vectors $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OQ}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OR}}$ each of magnitude $\mathrm{A}$ are acting as shown in figure. The resultant of the three vectors is $\mathrm{A} \sqrt{x}$. The value of $x$ is ________ .
question: Three vectors $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OQ}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OR}}$ each of magnitude $\mathrm{A}$ are acting as shown in figure. The resultant of the three vectors is $\mathrm{A} \sqrt{x}$. The value of $x$ is ________ .
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \vec{R}=\left(A+\frac{A}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \hat{i}+\left(A-\frac{A}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \hat{j} \\ & |\vec{R}|=\sqrt{\left(A+\frac{A}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2+\left(A-\frac{A}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2}=\sqrt{3} A\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
The maximum percentage error in the measurment of density of a wire is [Given, mass of wire $=(0.60 \pm 0.003) \mathrm{g}$
radius of wire $=(0.50 \pm 0.01) \mathrm{cm}$
length of wire $=(10.00 \pm 0.05) \mathrm{cm}]$
question:
The maximum percentage error in the measurment of density of a wire is [Given, mass of wire $=(0.60 \pm 0.003) \mathrm{g}$
radius of wire $=(0.50 \pm 0.01) \mathrm{cm}$
length of wire $=(10.00 \pm 0.05) \mathrm{cm}]$
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{d}=\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\text { vol. }}=\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\pi \mathrm{R}^2 \ell} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} \rho}{\rho}=\frac{\mathrm{dm}}{\mathrm{m}}+\frac{2 \mathrm{dR}}{\mathrm{R}}+\frac{\mathrm{d} \ell}{\ell} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} \rho}{\rho}=\left(\frac{0.003}{0.6}+\frac{2 \times 0.01}{0.5}+\frac{0.05}{10}\right) 100=5 \%\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The amount of heat produced in an electric circuit depends upon the current $(I)$, resistance $(R)$ and time $(t)$. If the error made in the measurements of the above quantities are $2 \%, 1 \%$ and $1 \%$ respectively then the maximum possible error in the total heat produced will be
question: The amount of heat produced in an electric circuit depends upon the current $(I)$, resistance $(R)$ and time $(t)$. If the error made in the measurements of the above quantities are $2 \%, 1 \%$ and $1 \%$ respectively then the maximum possible error in the total heat produced will be
solution:
Given : $\frac{\Delta I}{I} \times 100=2 \%$;
$\frac{\Delta R}{R} \times 100=1 \%$ and $\frac{\Delta t}{t} \times 100=1 \%$
Heat produced, $H=I^2 R t$
Maximum possible error in heat produced,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{\Delta H}{H} \times 100 \\
& =2\left(\frac{\Delta I}{I} \times 100\right)+\frac{\Delta R}{R} \times 100+\frac{\Delta t}{t} \times 100 \\
& =2 \times 2 \%+1 \%+1 \%=6 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: Two vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>X</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">Y</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">→</mo></mover></math> have equal magnitude. The magnitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">X</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">→</mo></mover><mo mathvariant="italic">-</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">Y</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">→</mo></mover><mo>)</mo></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> times the magnitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">X</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">→</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi>Y</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>)</mo></math>. The angle between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>X</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">Y</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">→</mo></mover></math> is :
question: Two vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>X</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">Y</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">→</mo></mover></math> have equal magnitude. The magnitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">X</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">→</mo></mover><mo mathvariant="italic">-</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">Y</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">→</mo></mover><mo>)</mo></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> times the magnitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">X</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">→</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi>Y</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>)</mo></math>. The angle between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>X</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">Y</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">→</mo></mover></math> is :
solution:
Given X=Y

X2+Y2-2×Ycosθ

=nX2+Y2+2×Ycosθ

Square both sides

2X2(1-cosθ)=n2·2X2(1+cosθ)

1-cosθ=n2+n2cosθ

cosθ=1-n21+n2

θ=cos-1n2-1-n2-1

subject: Physics
q: An experiment is performed to obtain the value of acceleration due to gravity g by using a simple pendulum of length L. In this experiment time for 100 oscillations is measured  by using a watch of 1 second least count and the value is 90.0 seconds. The length L is measured by using a meter scale of least count 1 mm and the value is 20.0 cm. The error in the determination of g would be :
question: An experiment is performed to obtain the value of acceleration due to gravity g by using a simple pendulum of length L. In this experiment time for 100 oscillations is measured  by using a watch of 1 second least count and the value is 90.0 seconds. The length L is measured by using a meter scale of least count 1 mm and the value is 20.0 cm. The error in the determination of g would be :
solution:
T = 2 π L g

∴    T 2 = 4 π 2 L g

∴     g = 4 π 2 L T 2

% Error in g = Δ L L 0 × 1 0 0 + 2 Δ T T 0 × 1 0 0

                   = 1 × 1 0 - 3 0.20 × 1 0 0 + 2 1 9 0 × 1 0 0

                   = 0.5 + 2 1 0 9

                   = 0.5 + 2 0 9

                   = 0.5 + 2.22

                  = 2.7%

subject: Physics
q: Young's modulus is determined by the equation given by $\mathrm{Y}=49000 \frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{l}} \frac{\mathrm{dyn}}{\mathrm{cm}^2}$ where $M$ is the mass and $l$ is the extension of wire used in the experiment. Now error in Young modules $(Y)$ is estimated by taking data from $M-l$ plot in graph paper. The smallest scale divisions are $5 \mathrm{~g}$ and $0.02 \mathrm{~cm}$ along load axis and extension axis respectively. If the value of $M$ and $l$ are $500 \mathrm{~g}$ and $2 \mathrm{~cm}$ respectively then percentage error of $Y$ is :
question: Young's modulus is determined by the equation given by $\mathrm{Y}=49000 \frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{l}} \frac{\mathrm{dyn}}{\mathrm{cm}^2}$ where $M$ is the mass and $l$ is the extension of wire used in the experiment. Now error in Young modules $(Y)$ is estimated by taking data from $M-l$ plot in graph paper. The smallest scale divisions are $5 \mathrm{~g}$ and $0.02 \mathrm{~cm}$ along load axis and extension axis respectively. If the value of $M$ and $l$ are $500 \mathrm{~g}$ and $2 \mathrm{~cm}$ respectively then percentage error of $Y$ is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} \frac{\Delta \mathrm{Y}}{\mathrm{Y}} & =\frac{\Delta \mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{m}}+\frac{\Delta \ell}{\ell} \\ & =\frac{5}{500}+\frac{0.02}{2}=0.01+0.01 \\ \frac{\Delta \mathrm{Y}}{\mathrm{Y}} & =0.02 \Rightarrow \% \frac{\Delta \mathrm{Y}}{\mathrm{Y}}=2 \%\end{aligned}$
